title: Handler, Looper & MessageQueue
description: The threading backbone of Android: Looper event loops, MessageQueue scheduling, Handler dispatch, and sync barriers
lesson:
The Core Mental Model: Thread + Looper + MessageQueue + Handler

Every Android app is built on top of one fundamental pattern: a thread running an infinite loop that pulls messages from a queue and dispatches them. This is how the UI thread works, and it is how you can make any thread work the same way.

Thread │ └── Looper (infinite loop) │ └── MessageQueue (priority queue, sorted by timestamp) │ ├── Message { what=1, obj=data, target=handler, when=T } ├── Message { callback=Runnable, target=handler, when=T+50 } └── Message { what=2, arg1=42, target=handler, when=T+100 } │ ▼ Handler (sends messages IN, dispatches them OUT) │ ├── sendMessage(msg) → enqueue ├── post(runnable) → enqueue as callback └── handleMessage(msg) → called by Looper when dequeued
Key insight: A Handler is always bound to exactly ONE Looper (and its thread). When you post a message through a Handler, the message runs on that Handler's Looper thread, not the thread you called post() from. This is what makes cross-thread communication work.

How the Main Thread Works

The main thread is not special magic. It is a regular Java thread with a Looper attached. Here is (simplified) what ActivityThread.main() does when your app starts:

// Simplified ActivityThread.main() — the REAL entry point of every Android app public static void main(String[] args) { // 1. Create the Looper + MessageQueue for this thread Looper.prepareMainLooper(); // 2. Create the ActivityThread (manages Activity lifecycle) ActivityThread thread = new ActivityThread(); thread.attach(false); // 3. Get the main Handler (H class) for receiving system messages if (sMainThreadHandler == null) { sMainThreadHandler = thread.getHandler(); } // 4. Start the infinite loop — this call NEVER returns Looper.loop(); // ← blocks forever, processing messages throw new RuntimeException("Main thread loop unexpectedly exited"); }

Every lifecycle callback (onCreate, onResume, onDraw) is just a Message processed by this loop. When people say "don't block the main thread," they mean: don't do slow work inside a message handler, because the next message (e.g., drawing the next frame) is waiting in the queue.

Main Thread Timeline: ┌─────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌─────────┐ │ onCreate │ │ onResume │ │ INPUT │ │ DRAW │ │ INPUT │ │ Activity │ │ Activity │ │ touch │ │ frame │ │ key │ └─────────┘ └──────────┘ └─────────┘ └──────────┘ └─────────┘ ▲ ▲ ▲ ▲ ▲ │ │ │ │ │ ─────┴─────────────┴────────────┴─────────────┴────────────┴────▶ time msg1 msg2 msg3 msg4 msg5 Looper.loop() pulls one message at a time, sequentially. If msg3 takes 500ms → frame drop (ANR if >5s)

MessageQueue: Priority Queue + epoll Blocking

MessageQueue is a singly linked list sorted by when timestamp (not FIFO — it is a time-ordered priority queue). Each Message has a when field indicating the earliest time it should be dispatched.

// Simplified MessageQueue.enqueueMessage() boolean enqueueMessage(Message msg, long when) { synchronized (this) { msg.when = when; Message p = mMessages; // head of linked list // Insert at head if queue is empty, or new msg is earliest if (p == null || when == 0 || when < p.when) { msg.next = p; mMessages = msg; needWake = true; // wake the blocked thread } else { // Walk the list to find insertion point (sorted by when) Message prev; for (;;) { prev = p; p = p.next; if (p == null || when < p.when) break; } msg.next = p; prev.next = msg; } if (needWake) { nativeWake(mPtr); // write to eventfd → wakes epoll } } } // Simplified MessageQueue.next() — called by Looper.loop() Message next() { for (;;) { nativePollOnce(mPtr, nextPollTimeoutMillis); // ↑ blocks on epoll_wait() until timeout or nativeWake() synchronized (this) { Message msg = mMessages; if (msg != null && msg.when <= now) { mMessages = msg.next; return msg; // dispatch this message } // Not ready yet — compute how long to sleep nextPollTimeoutMillis = (int)(msg.when - now); } } }
Why epoll? The main thread cannot busy-wait (that would burn CPU). Instead, nativePollOnce() calls Linux epoll_wait() which puts the thread to sleep with zero CPU cost. When a new message arrives, nativeWake() writes a byte to an eventfd, which wakes the epoll. This is why the main thread uses 0% CPU when idle.

Handler: The Interface to the Queue

A Handler does two things: (1) enqueue messages into the MessageQueue, and (2) dispatch them when the Looper pulls them out. Every message carries a reference to the Handler that sent it (the target field).

// Creating a Handler — ALWAYS specify a Looper explicitly (API 30+) val mainHandler = Handler(Looper.getMainLooper()) // ── Sending messages ── // Option 1: post a Runnable (wraps it in a Message internally) mainHandler.post { textView.text = "Updated from background thread" } // Option 2: postDelayed — runs after a delay mainHandler.postDelayed({ showTimeout() }, 5000) // 5 seconds from now // Option 3: sendMessage — for structured message passing val msg = Message.obtain(mainHandler, WHAT_UPDATE, dataObject) mainHandler.sendMessage(msg) // Option 4: sendMessageAtFrontOfQueue — skip ahead (rare, use carefully) mainHandler.sendMessageAtFrontOfQueue(urgentMsg)
// Handling messages — override handleMessage for structured messages class MyHandler(looper: Looper) : Handler(looper) { override fun handleMessage(msg: Message) { when (msg.what) { WHAT_UPDATE -> { val data = msg.obj as MyData updateUI(data) } WHAT_ERROR -> showError(msg.arg1) } } } // Under the hood, Looper.loop() does this: // msg.target.dispatchMessage(msg) // dispatchMessage checks: // 1. msg.callback != null? → run the Runnable // 2. mCallback != null? → Handler.Callback.handleMessage() // 3. else → this.handleMessage(msg)
Memory leak trap: Never use Handler() (no-arg constructor, deprecated). An inner class Handler holds an implicit reference to the outer Activity. If a delayed message is in the queue when the Activity is destroyed, the Handler keeps the Activity alive → memory leak. Solutions: (1) use a static inner class with a WeakReference, (2) call handler.removeCallbacksAndMessages(null) in onDestroy, or (3) use lifecycleScope instead.

Message Pooling: obtain() vs new Message()

Creating Message objects generates garbage. Android solves this with an object pool — a linked list of recycled Message objects. Always use Message.obtain() instead of new Message().

// WRONG — allocates a new object, creates GC pressure val msg = Message() msg.what = 42 handler.sendMessage(msg) // RIGHT — reuses a recycled Message from the pool val msg = Message.obtain(handler, 42) // sets target + what handler.sendMessage(msg) // ALSO RIGHT — handler.obtainMessage() is a convenience wrapper val msg = handler.obtainMessage(42, dataObj) handler.sendMessage(msg)
Message Pool (singly linked list, max 50): sPool → [Message] → [Message] → [Message] → null │ ▼ obtain() → takes head, resets all fields, returns it recycle() → clears fields, pushes onto head Looper.loop() calls msg.recycleUnchecked() AUTOMATICALLY after dispatch — you do NOT need to recycle manually.
Pool size limit: The pool holds a maximum of 50 Messages (MAX_POOL_SIZE = 50). Under heavy messaging, some Messages will be GC'd, but the pool keeps the common case allocation-free.

HandlerThread: A Thread with a Built-In Looper

Normally you need boilerplate to set up a Looper on a background thread. HandlerThread does it for you — it calls Looper.prepare() and Looper.loop() automatically.

// Creating a background thread with its own message queue val handlerThread = HandlerThread("MyBackgroundThread") handlerThread.start() // Looper.prepare() + Looper.loop() happen inside // Create a Handler attached to that thread's Looper val bgHandler = Handler(handlerThread.looper) // Post work to the background thread bgHandler.post { // This runs on "MyBackgroundThread", NOT the main thread val result = heavyComputation() // Switch back to main thread to update UI mainHandler.post { textView.text = result } } // IMPORTANT: quit when done to avoid thread leaks handlerThread.quitSafely() // processes remaining messages, then exits // vs handlerThread.quit() — drops pending messages immediately
Use cases for HandlerThread:
- Serial background work (database writes, logging) where order matters
- Long-lived worker threads (sensor polling, Bluetooth communication)
- IntentService uses a HandlerThread internally
- Whenever you need a dedicated thread with a message queue

Sync Barriers and Choreographer

A sync barrier is a special Message with target == null. When the Looper encounters it, it skips all synchronous messages and only dispatches asynchronous ones. This is how Android ensures that drawing commands get priority over everything else.

Normal queue processing: [msg1] → [msg2] → [msg3] → dispatch in order With sync barrier: [BARRIER] → [msg1:sync] → [msg2:ASYNC] → [msg3:sync] → [msg4:ASYNC] skip! ▲ skip! ▲ dispatch dispatch Barrier blocks ALL sync messages. Only async messages pass through. Barrier must be removed explicitly via removeSyncBarrier().
// How Choreographer uses sync barriers (simplified): // 1. VSYNC signal arrives → Choreographer posts a barrier MessageQueue queue = Looper.myQueue(); int token = queue.postSyncBarrier(); // blocks sync msgs // 2. Choreographer posts its traversal as an ASYNC message Message msg = Message.obtain(handler, traversalRunnable); msg.setAsynchronous(true); // this msg passes through the barrier handler.sendMessage(msg); // 3. Traversal runs (measure/layout/draw) with priority // 4. After traversal, barrier is removed queue.removeSyncBarrier(token); // Now sync messages flow again
Danger: If you post a sync barrier and forget to remove it, the main thread freezes — all regular messages (input events, lifecycle callbacks) are blocked forever. This is a real source of hard-to-debug ANRs. The postSyncBarrier() API is @hide for this reason — you cannot call it from app code without reflection.

The Classic Async Pattern: Background Thread to Main Thread

Before coroutines and RxJava, this Handler pattern was the standard way to do background work and update the UI. Understanding it is essential because it is what coroutines compile down to under the hood.

// The classic pre-coroutine async pattern class MyActivity : AppCompatActivity() { private val mainHandler = Handler(Looper.getMainLooper()) private lateinit var bgThread: HandlerThread private lateinit var bgHandler: Handler override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) // Set up background thread bgThread = HandlerThread("NetworkThread") bgThread.start() bgHandler = Handler(bgThread.looper) loadData() } private fun loadData() { showLoading() // Step 1: Run on background thread bgHandler.post { val result = try { api.fetchUser(userId) // blocking network call — OK here } catch (e: Exception) { null } // Step 2: Switch back to main thread for UI update mainHandler.post { if (result != null) { showUser(result) } else { showError() } } } } override fun onDestroy() { super.onDestroy() // CRITICAL: prevent leaks and stale callbacks bgHandler.removeCallbacksAndMessages(null) mainHandler.removeCallbacksAndMessages(null) bgThread.quitSafely() } }
Modern equivalent: The Handler pattern above is exactly what Dispatchers.Main and Dispatchers.IO do under the hood. Dispatchers.Main posts Runnables to a Handler bound to the main Looper. withContext(Dispatchers.IO) posts work to an IO thread pool. Coroutines are syntactic sugar over Handler + thread pools.

Looper Internals: prepare() and loop()

You can attach a Looper to any thread. The two key methods are prepare() (creates the Looper and MessageQueue) and loop() (starts the infinite dispatch loop).

// How to turn any thread into a looper thread (what HandlerThread does) Thread { Looper.prepare() // creates Looper + MessageQueue, stores in ThreadLocal val handler = Handler(Looper.myLooper()!!) // Post an initial task handler.post { doSomething() } Looper.loop() // blocks forever, processing messages // Code here is unreachable unless quit() is called }.start() // ThreadLocal storage — each thread gets its own Looper // Looper.myLooper() returns the Looper for the CURRENT thread // Looper.getMainLooper() returns the main thread's Looper (global)
Rules:
- Looper.prepare() can only be called once per thread. Calling it twice throws RuntimeException.
- Looper.prepareMainLooper() can only be called once per app. The framework calls it — you never should.
- A thread without Looper.prepare() cannot create a Handler. You will get: "Can't create handler inside thread that has not called Looper.prepare()"

Quick Reference: Message Fields

FieldTypePurpose
whatintMessage type identifier (your custom constant)
arg1, arg2intLightweight int args (no allocation)
objObjectArbitrary data payload
dataBundleExtra data (lazily allocated)
targetHandlerWhich Handler dispatches this message
callbackRunnableIf set, run this instead of handleMessage()
whenlongTimestamp for when to dispatch (uptimeMillis)
nextMessageNext message in linked list (queue or pool)

How Touch Input Travels from Hardware to Your App

Touch events do not magically appear in your onTouchEvent(). They travel through a multi-layer pipeline that spans hardware, the Linux kernel, a native system service, a socket, and finally the main thread Looper. Understanding this pipeline explains why blocking the main thread makes touch feel broken.

The Full Input Pipeline: ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ HARDWARE │ │ Touch panel generates interrupt │ └──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ LINUX KERNEL │ │ Input driver handles interrupt → writes to /dev/input/eventX │ └──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ SYSTEM_SERVER (native) │ │ InputReader: reads raw events from /dev/input/eventX │ │ InputDispatcher: determines which window receives the event │ └──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘ ▼ (sent via unix socket pair) ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ YOUR APP PROCESS (main thread) │ │ InputChannel (socket fd registered with Looper's epoll) │ │ ▼ │ │ ViewRootImpl.WindowInputEventReceiver.onInputEvent() │ │ ▼ │ │ Activity.dispatchTouchEvent() │ │ ▼ │ │ Window → DecorView │ │ ▼ │ │ ViewGroup.dispatchTouchEvent() (recursive hit testing) │ │ ▼ │ │ View.onTouchEvent() ← YOUR CODE │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Key insight: Input delivery is just another message on the main thread Looper. The InputEventReceiver bridges the system's input socket to the Looper's MessageQueue — when data arrives on the socket, the Looper's epoll wakes up and the receiver's onInputEvent() is called, which dispatches the touch through the View hierarchy. There is no separate "input thread" in your app.
What happens when the main thread is blocked: If your main thread is doing a 3-second database query, input events queue up in the socket buffer. The InputDispatcher in system_server tracks how long it has been waiting for your app to acknowledge the event. If an input event is not consumed within 5 seconds, the system shows the ANR (Application Not Responding) dialog.

The relationship between input and the 16ms frame budget is critical: the Looper processes messages sequentially. If your onTouchEvent() or any other message handler takes 20ms, the next VSYNC frame callback has to wait. The touch event is processed, but the visual response is delayed — the user perceives this as "laggy" touch because the screen does not update until the next available frame.

How Choreographer Drives Animations and Rendering

Choreographer is the coordinator between VSYNC signals from the display hardware and your app's main thread. It ensures that animations, layout, and drawing all happen in sync with the display's refresh cycle.

// Choreographer schedules work aligned to VSYNC // When VSYNC arrives, doFrame() is called with four callback phases: // Phase 1: CALLBACK_INPUT // Process pending input events (touches, keys) // Phase 2: CALLBACK_ANIMATION // Tick ValueAnimator, ObjectAnimator, Transition animations // Phase 3: CALLBACK_INSETS_ANIMATION // Window inset animations (keyboard show/hide, system bars) // Phase 4: CALLBACK_TRAVERSAL // View.requestLayout() triggers this: measure → layout → draw // Using postFrameCallback to run code synchronized with VSYNC: Choreographer.getInstance().postFrameCallback { frameTimeNanos -> // This runs at the start of the next frame // frameTimeNanos = the VSYNC timestamp val elapsed = frameTimeNanos - startTime updateAnimation(elapsed) }
Frame Timeline (16ms budget at 60fps): VSYNC VSYNC │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌──────┬────────────┬──────────────┬───────────┐ │INPUT │ ANIMATION │ TRAVERSAL │RenderThread│ │ │ │measure/layout│ + GPU │ │ │ │ /draw │ │ └──────┴────────────┴──────────────┴───────────┘ │◄──────── main thread ──────────►│◄── RT ────►│ │◄──────────────── must complete before next VSYNC ──────►│ If total time > 16ms → missed VSYNC → dropped frame → JANK Choreographer.doFrame() timing: - Measures frame time at start - If frame is already late (missed VSYNC), logs: "Skipped N frames! The application may be doing too much work on its main thread."
How postFrameCallback works: When you call Choreographer.getInstance().postFrameCallback(callback), the callback is registered for the next VSYNC. When VSYNC arrives, Choreographer's doFrame() runs all registered callbacks in phase order. Your frame callback runs synchronized with the display refresh — this is how smooth animations work. The callback receives the exact VSYNC timestamp so animations can be frame-rate independent.

The Relationship Between MessageQueue, VSYNC, and Sync Barriers

Now we can see the full picture of how sync barriers, Choreographer, and the MessageQueue work together to prioritize rendering.

// What happens when Choreographer schedules a frame (simplified): // 1. VSYNC signal arrives from display hardware // 2. Choreographer posts a sync barrier to the MessageQueue int token = mQueue.postSyncBarrier(); // 3. Choreographer posts doFrame() as an ASYNC message Message msg = Message.obtain(mHandler, mFrameRunnable); msg.setAsynchronous(true); // passes through the barrier msg.setWhen(vsyncTime); mHandler.sendMessageAtTime(msg, vsyncTime); // 4. The MessageQueue now looks like this: // Regular messages are BLOCKED by the barrier // Only the async doFrame message passes through // 5. doFrame() runs: input → animation → traversal // 6. After frame processing, the barrier is removed mQueue.removeSyncBarrier(token); // 7. Regular messages (Handler.post(), LiveData updates, etc.) resume
MessageQueue during a frame: BEFORE VSYNC: [Handler.post #1] → [LiveData update] → [Handler.post #2] All messages process normally in order. AFTER VSYNC (barrier posted): [BARRIER] → [Handler.post #1] → [ASYNC: doFrame()] → [LiveData update] → [Handler.post #2] │ ✗ blocked ✓ passes! ✗ blocked ✗ blocked │ │ │ ▼ │ Choreographer.doFrame() │ input → anim → traversal │ │ │ ▼ └──── BARRIER REMOVED ──── regular messages resume ────▶ [Handler.post #1] → [LiveData update] → [Handler.post #2] ✓ now processes ✓ ✓ This is WHY animations and input feel responsive even when the main thread has pending work — they literally skip the queue.
Sync barriers in context: This mechanism is why a Handler.post { } callback or a LiveData observer update can be slightly delayed during active animations — the sync barrier temporarily holds them back while the frame is processed. It is also why a forgotten sync barrier (leaked by framework bugs) causes the app to freeze: all regular messages are blocked indefinitely.

Why requestLayout() and invalidate() Don't Happen Immediately

A common misconception is that calling requestLayout() or invalidate() immediately triggers measure, layout, or draw. They do not. They schedule work for the next VSYNC frame via Choreographer.

// What requestLayout() actually does (simplified): void requestLayout() { mPrivateFlags |= PFLAG_FORCE_LAYOUT; // mark this view as needing layout if (mParent != null) { mParent.requestLayout(); // propagate up to ViewRootImpl } } // ViewRootImpl.requestLayout() schedules a traversal: void requestLayout() { if (!mHandlingLayoutInLayoutRequest) { scheduleTraversals(); // asks Choreographer for a VSYNC callback } } void scheduleTraversals() { if (!mTraversalScheduled) { mTraversalScheduled = true; // Post a sync barrier (block regular messages) mTraversalBarrier = mHandler.getLooper().getQueue().postSyncBarrier(); // Schedule traversal for next VSYNC mChoreographer.postCallback( Choreographer.CALLBACK_TRAVERSAL, mTraversalRunnable, null); } } // Key: mTraversalScheduled flag prevents duplicate scheduling // Multiple requestLayout() calls → only ONE traversal per frame
Common bug — reading stale layout values:
textView.text = "New text" // internally calls requestLayout()
val width = textView.width // STALE! Still has the old width

Since requestLayout() defers the actual measure/layout to the next VSYNC, reading width or height immediately after gives you the old values. To get post-layout values, use:
textView.post { val width = textView.width } — runs after the next layout pass.
Or use textView.doOnLayout { val width = it.width } from AndroidX.
Why batching matters: You can call requestLayout() 100 times in a loop and it will not freeze the app. The mTraversalScheduled flag in ViewRootImpl ensures that only one traversal is scheduled per frame. All 100 calls just set dirty flags on views — the single traversal pass handles them all. This is a deliberate design that separates "marking what needs updating" from "actually doing the update."

Looper, ANR, and StrictMode

ANR stands for Application Not Responding. It means the main thread Looper did not process a pending event within the system's timeout. Understanding the exact mechanism helps you avoid it.

ANR Detection Mechanism: system_server (InputDispatcher) Your App (main thread) │ │ │── dispatch touch event via socket ───────▶│ │ │ (blocked doing │ starts 5-second timer │ database query) │ │ │ waiting for acknowledgment... │ │ │ │ 5 seconds elapsed, no response │ │ │ ▼ │ ANR DIALOG shown to user │ "App isn't responding" │ [Wait] [Close app] │ ANR Timeouts: Input event dispatch: 5 seconds BroadcastReceiver: 10 seconds (foreground) 60 seconds (background) Service.onCreate: 20 seconds
// Common ANR causes: // 1. Database query on main thread fun onClick(v: View) { val users = db.userDao().getAllUsers() // BLOCKS main thread! adapter.submitList(users) } // 2. Synchronous network call on main thread fun onResume() { val response = httpClient.execute(request) // ANR if server slow } // 3. Deadlock with background thread fun onTouchEvent(event: MotionEvent): Boolean { synchronized(lock) { // waits for background thread to release lock processTouch(event) } return true } // 4. Heavy computation fun onBindViewHolder(holder: ViewHolder, position: Int) { val bitmap = decodeLargeImage(items[position].path) // too slow! holder.imageView.setImageBitmap(bitmap) }
// StrictMode: detect ANR causes BEFORE they become ANRs if (BuildConfig.DEBUG) { StrictMode.setThreadPolicy( StrictMode.ThreadPolicy.Builder() .detectDiskReads() // flag SharedPreferences reads, file I/O .detectDiskWrites() // flag database writes, file saves .detectNetwork() // flag any network call .detectCustomSlowCalls() // flag your own slow operations .penaltyLog() // log violations (use in debug) .penaltyDeath() // crash on violation (aggressive) .build() ) } // StrictMode works by hooking into the Looper: // It sets Looper.setMessageLogging() to measure how long each // message takes to dispatch. If a message involves disk/network // I/O on the main thread, StrictMode flags it.
How StrictMode hooks the Looper: StrictMode installs a Printer via Looper.setMessageLogging(). The Looper logs ">>>>> Dispatching" before each message and "<<<<< Finished" after. StrictMode intercepts these to measure dispatch time and detect I/O calls. This is also how third-party tools like BlockCanary detect jank — they measure the time between "Dispatching" and "Finished" for each message.

questions.title: Handler Thread Binding
questions.description: A Handler is created on a background thread with Handler(Looper.getMainLooper()). When you call handler.post { updateTextView() } from that background thread, where does updateTextView() execute?
options: On the background thread that called post()
On the main (UI) thread
On a new thread created by the Handler
It throws an exception because you can't create a Handler from a background thread
questions.explanation: A Handler is bound to the Looper passed to its constructor, not the thread that creates it. Since Looper.getMainLooper() is the main thread's Looper, the Runnable is enqueued into the main thread's MessageQueue and dispatched on the main thread. This is the fundamental mechanism for cross-thread communication in Android.
hints: The Handler is bound to a Looper, not to the thread that created it
Think about which thread's MessageQueue the Runnable goes into

questions.title: Message Ordering
questions.description: What is the output order and why?
options: A, B, C, D (insertion order)
B, D, C, A (immediate messages first, then by delay)
D, B, C, A (reverse order)
B, C, D, A (random order, threading is unpredictable)
questions.explanation: MessageQueue is sorted by when timestamp. post() sets when = now, so B and D have the earliest timestamps and run first (in insertion order since they have effectively equal times). postDelayed(100) puts C at now+100ms, and postDelayed(300) puts A at now+300ms. Result: B, D, C, A.
hints: MessageQueue is not FIFO — it's sorted by timestamp
post() is equivalent to postDelayed(runnable, 0)

questions.title: Handler Memory Leak
questions.description: This code leaks the Activity. When the user rotates the device, the old Activity is destroyed but cannot be garbage collected. Fix the leak.

checks.message: Remove pending messages in onDestroy to break the reference chain

questions.explanation: When the Activity is destroyed (rotation), any pending messages in the MessageQueue still hold a reference to the Handler, which holds a reference to the Activity (via the lambda capturing this). Calling handler.removeCallbacksAndMessages(null) in onDestroy() clears all pending messages, breaking the reference chain and allowing the Activity to be garbage collected.
hints: The delayed Runnable captures a reference to the Activity
Messages stay in the queue even after the Activity is destroyed
There's a Handler method that removes ALL pending work

questions.title: Message.obtain() vs new Message()
questions.description: Why should you use Message.obtain() instead of new Message()?
options: Message.obtain() is thread-safe, new Message() is not
Message.obtain() recycles from an object pool, avoiding garbage collection pressure
new Message() is deprecated and throws an exception on API 30+
Message.obtain() runs the message immediately without queuing
questions.explanation: Android maintains a pool of up to 50 recycled Message objects in a singly linked list. Message.obtain() grabs one from the pool and resets its fields, avoiding allocation. After the Looper dispatches a message, it automatically returns it to the pool. On a busy main thread processing 60+ messages per second (every frame), this prevents significant GC pressure.
hints: Think about what happens when you create thousands of short-lived objects
Android's main thread processes a LOT of messages — one per frame minimum

questions.title: HandlerThread Setup
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create a background HandlerThread, post work to it, and clean up properly.

blanks.hint: The convenience class that wraps Thread + Looper
You need to start the thread before using its Looper
The property that returns the thread's Looper after start()
Stop the looper to avoid leaking the thread

questions.explanation: HandlerThread is a Thread subclass that calls Looper.prepare() and Looper.loop() in its run() method. After calling start(), you access its Looper via the looper property and create a Handler bound to it. Always call quitSafely() when done — it lets pending messages finish, then exits the loop. Without quit, the thread runs forever.
hints: Think about the class name that combines Handler + Thread
A thread must be started before you can use it
The HandlerThread exposes its Looper as a property
quitSafely() processes remaining messages before stopping

questions.title: Sync Barriers and Choreographer
questions.description: What is a sync barrier in the MessageQueue, and why does Choreographer use one?
options: A sync barrier pauses ALL messages until a condition is met, used for thread synchronization
A sync barrier blocks synchronous messages and only lets asynchronous messages pass — Choreographer uses it to give drawing commands priority over other work
A sync barrier prevents multiple threads from posting messages simultaneously
A sync barrier is a lock that ensures messages are processed in FIFO order
questions.explanation: A sync barrier is a special Message with target == null. When the Looper encounters it, it skips all normal (synchronous) messages and only dispatches messages marked as asynchronous (msg.setAsynchronous(true)). Choreographer posts a sync barrier when VSYNC arrives, then posts its traversal (measure/layout/draw) as an async message. This ensures drawing gets priority over input events, callbacks, and other queued work — critical for maintaining 60fps.
hints: Think about what happens to non-drawing messages when the screen needs to render
The barrier filters messages based on a flag, not timing

questions.title: Looper.prepare() Error
questions.description: What happens when this code runs?
options: Prints "Hello" on the new thread
Prints "Hello" on the main thread
Crashes with NullPointerException — Looper.myLooper() returns null because Looper.prepare() was never called
Crashes with RuntimeException: "Can't create handler inside thread that has not called Looper.prepare()"
questions.explanation: A new thread does not have a Looper by default. Looper.myLooper() returns null for a thread that has not called Looper.prepare(). The !! operator then throws a NullPointerException. The fix: either call Looper.prepare() before creating the Handler, use HandlerThread, or pass Looper.getMainLooper() if you want the main thread.
hints: By default, only the main thread has a Looper
What does Looper.myLooper() return on a fresh thread?

questions.title: Background to Main Thread Communication
questions.description: This code crashes with "Only the original thread that created a view hierarchy can touch its views." Fix it using a Handler.

checks.message: Use Handler(Looper.getMainLooper()) or runOnUiThread to switch to the UI thread
Target the main thread's Looper for UI updates

questions.explanation: Android's View system is single-threaded — only the main (UI) thread can modify views. The fix creates a Handler bound to Looper.getMainLooper(), then uses handler.post { } to enqueue the UI update onto the main thread's MessageQueue. The Looper on the main thread picks it up and runs it safely. Alternatively, runOnUiThread { } (which uses a Handler internally) works too.
hints: You need to move the UI update to the main thread
A Handler bound to the main Looper can post work to the main thread
The network call should stay on the background thread

questions.title: requestLayout() Coalescing
questions.description: requestLayout() is called 100 times in a tight loop on the main thread. How many measure/layout passes actually happen?
options: 100 — one for each call
1 — they coalesce via Choreographer's traversal scheduling
0 — requestLayout() in a loop is a no-op
It depends on how fast the loop runs relative to VSYNC
questions.explanation: Only 1 measure/layout pass happens. The first requestLayout() call propagates up to ViewRootImpl, which calls scheduleTraversals(). This sets mTraversalScheduled = true and posts a callback to Choreographer for the next VSYNC. All subsequent requestLayout() calls see that mTraversalScheduled is already true and skip scheduling again. Each call still marks its View as needing layout (sets the PFLAG_FORCE_LAYOUT flag), but the actual traversal — measure, layout, draw — happens exactly once on the next VSYNC frame.
hints: Think about what scheduleTraversals() does when called multiple times
There is a flag that prevents duplicate scheduling

questions.title: Leaked Sync Barrier
questions.description: A sync barrier is posted to the MessageQueue but never removed. What happens to the app?
options: Nothing — barriers expire after a timeout
The app crashes with a RuntimeException
All regular (synchronous) messages are blocked forever — the app appears frozen, but async messages like Choreographer's VSYNC frame callbacks may still run
The Looper detects the stuck barrier and removes it automatically
questions.explanation: A sync barrier has no timeout and is never removed automatically. Once posted, it blocks all synchronous messages — Handler.post() calls, LiveData updates, lifecycle callbacks, and input event dispatch. The app appears completely frozen to the user. However, asynchronous messages (those with setAsynchronous(true)) still pass through the barrier, so Choreographer's frame callbacks might still run, meaning animations could theoretically continue even though the app is otherwise unresponsive. This is a real source of hard-to-debug ANRs. The postSyncBarrier() API is @hide precisely because a leaked barrier is so dangerous.
hints: Sync barriers are explicit — they must be removed with removeSyncBarrier()
Think about which messages are sync vs async

questions.title: Slow onTouchEvent Impact
questions.description: Touch input takes 20ms to process in onTouchEvent(). The display runs at 60fps (16ms per frame). What does the user experience?
options: Nothing noticeable — 20ms is close enough to 16ms
The next frame is delayed because the Looper is still processing the touch event, causing a dropped frame — touch feels laggy. If sustained, could eventually trigger an ANR.
The touch event is dropped and the user's input is lost
The system automatically moves touch processing to a background thread
questions.explanation: The main thread Looper processes messages sequentially. If onTouchEvent() takes 20ms, the Looper cannot process the next VSYNC frame callback until the touch handler finishes — but VSYNC arrives every 16ms. The frame that should have started at 16ms has to wait until 20ms, missing the VSYNC deadline. The display shows the previous frame again (dropped frame / jank). The user sees the visual response to their touch delayed by at least one frame. If every touch event takes 20ms, the app drops a frame on every touch, creating consistently "laggy" feeling input. If touch events back up for 5 seconds, the system triggers an ANR dialog.
hints: The Looper processes messages one at a time, sequentially
Think about what happens to the VSYNC frame callback while onTouchEvent is running

questions.title: ActivityThread.main()
options: It runs once to set up the Activity, then returns
It starts an infinite loop that pulls messages from the MessageQueue and dispatches them to their target Handlers — this is how ALL lifecycle callbacks, input events, and frame draws are processed
It creates a new thread for each incoming message
It polls the GPU for VSYNC signals at 60fps
questions.explanation: Looper.loop() is a for(;;) infinite loop. Each iteration calls MessageQueue.next() (which blocks on epoll_wait if the queue is empty), gets a Message, and calls msg.target.dispatchMessage(msg). Every single thing on the main thread — onCreate(), touch events, invalidate(), onDraw() — is a Message dispatched by this loop. The call to Looper.loop() never returns under normal operation.
hints: Think about what 'loop' means literally
How does the main thread keep running after onCreate() finishes?
